name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَحَفَفْنَـٰهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا (٣٢)
```

```english
Draw for them the parable of two men for each of whom We had made two gardens of vines, and We had surrounded them with date palms, and placed crops between them. (32)
```

```arabic
كِلْتَا ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ ءَاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا وَلَمْ تَظْلِم مِّنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَـٰلَهُمَا نَهَرًا (٣٣)
```

```english
Both gardens yielded their produce without stinting anything of it. And We had set a stream gushing through them. (33)
```

```arabic
وَكَانَ لَهُۥ ثَمَرٌ فَقَالَ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَنَا۠ أَكْثَرُ مِنكَ مَالًا وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا (٣٤)
```

```english
He had abundant fruits, so he said to his companion, as he conversed with him: ‘I have more wealth than you, and am stronger with respect to numbers.’ (34)
```

```arabic
وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُۥ وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ قَالَ مَآ أَظُنُّ أَن تَبِيدَ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا (٣٥)
```

```english
He entered his garden while he wronged himself. He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish, (35)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّدِدتُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا مُنقَلَبًا (٣٦)
```

```english
and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord I will surely find a resort better than this.’ (36)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَهُۥ صَاحِبُهُۥ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَكَفَرْتَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّىٰكَ رَجُلًا (٣٧)
```

```english
His companion said to him, as he conversed with him: ‘Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then fashioned you as a man? (37)
```

```arabic
لَّـٰكِنَّا۠ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا (٣٨)
```

```english
But I [say], ‘‘He is Allah, my Lord,’’ and I do not ascribe any partner to my Lord. (38)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن تَرَنِ أَنَا۠ أَقَلَّ مِنكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا (٣٩)
```

```english
Why did you not say, when you entered your garden, ‘‘[This is] as Allah has willed! There is no power except by Allah!’’ If you see that I have lesser wealth than you and children, (39)
```

```arabic
فَعَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يُؤْتِيَنِ خَيْرًا مِّن جَنَّتِكَ وَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهَا حُسْبَانًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتُصْبِحَ صَعِيدًا زَلَقًا (٤٠)
```

```english
maybe my Lord will give me [something] better than your garden, and He will unleash upon it bolts from the sky, so that it becomes a bare plain. (40)
```

```arabic
أَوْ يُصْبِحَ مَآؤُهَا غَوْرًا فَلَن تَسْتَطِيعَ لَهُۥ طَلَبًا (٤١)
```

```english
Or its water will sink down, so that you will never be able to obtain it.’ (41)
```

```arabic
وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِۦ فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنفَقَ فِيهَا وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا (٤٢)
```

```english
And ruin closed in on his produce, and he began to wring his hands for what he had spent on it, as it lay fallen on its trellises. He was saying, ‘I wish I had not ascribed any partner to my Lord.’ (42)
```

```arabic
وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ فِئَةٌ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مُنتَصِرًا (٤٣)
```

```english
He had no party to help him, besides Allah, nor could he help himself. (43)
```

```arabic
هُنَالِكَ ٱلْوَلَـٰيَةُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا (٤٤)
```

```english
There, all authority belongs to Allah, the Real. He is best in rewarding, and best in requiting. (44)
```

```arabic
وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحُ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا (٤٥)
```

```english
Draw for them the parable of the life of this world: [It is] like the water We send down from the sky. Then the earth’s vegetation mingles with it. Then it becomes chaff, scattered by the wind. And Allah has power over all things. (45)
```

```arabic
ٱلْمَالُ وَٱلْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلًا (٤٦)
```

```english
Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world, but lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward and better in hope. (46)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses contain two parables illustrating the reality of what man possesses in his worldly life of wealth and children. These are the ornaments and glitters of life—deceptive, swiftly vanishing, and fleeting—with which life beautifies itself for man, distracting him from the remembrance of his Lord and drawing his imagination toward eternalizing himself through them and relying upon them. Thus, he imagines that he owns them and has power over them, until a visitation from Allah, the Glorified, encompasses them, and they perish and vanish. Nothing remains for man from them except what resembles the dream of a sleeper or a false wish.

The verses return the discourse to clarifying the reality that He, the Glorified, alluded to in His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَهَا** (Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it...) until His saying: **صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا** (...a barren plain) [Qur'an 18:7-8].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلًا رَجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَابٍ** — **And draw for them the parable of two men: for one of them We made two gardens of vines...** [Qur'an 18:32]. That is, strike for these people who are infatuated with the glitter of the worldly life and turning away from the remembrance of Allah a parable, so it may become clear to them that they have attached themselves to nothing but an illusory mirage with no reality.

Some commentators have mentioned that what the parable contains is a hypothetical, assumed story, and it is not necessary for the content of the parable to have occurred externally. Others have stated that it is a factual story, and they have narrated many differing accounts regarding it which cannot be relied upon. However, contemplating the context of the story—including the mention of them being specifically two gardens, the restriction of their trees to vines and palms, the occurrence of crops between them, and other details—supports its being a factual story.

His saying: **جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَابٍ** (two gardens of vines) means of grapevines, for the fruit is often used to refer to its tree. His saying: **وَحَفَفْنَاهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ** (and We surrounded them with date palms) means We made the palms encompass them, bordering them from all sides. His saying: **وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا** (and We placed crops between them) means between the two gardens and in their midst. By this, the cultivation was continuous and complete, and both staples and fruits were gathered for him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كِلْتَا الْجَنَّتَيْنِ آتَتْ أُكُلَهَا** — **Both gardens yielded their produce...** [Qur'an 18:33]. The term *ukul* (أُكُل) refers to that which is eaten, and the meaning of the gardens yielding their produce is the fruiting of their trees, both vines and palms.

His saying: **وَلَمْ تَظْلِمْ مِنْهُ شَيْئًا** (and did not stint aught of it). Injustice (الظلم) here means deficiency (النقص). The pronoun refers to the produce (*ukul*); that is, the gardens did not diminish anything of their yield, but rather produced to the full extent of their capacity. His saying: **وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَالَهُمَا نَهَرًا** (and We made a river gush through them) means We cleaved a river of water through their midst to irrigate them and fulfill their need for drink by the easiest means without any burden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَانَ لَهُ ثَمَرٌ** — **He had [other] fruit...** [Qur'an 18:34]. The pronoun refers to the man, and *thamar* (ثَمَر) refers to types of wealth, as stated in *al-Sihah* and narrated from *al-Qamus*. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the palms and the *thamar* is their fruit. It was also said that it means the man had wealth he owned outside of his garden. The first of these views is the most appropriate, followed by the second. It is also possible that the meaning of the two gardens yielding their produce without stinting refers to their trees reaching the stage and season of fruiting, while His saying: **وَكَانَ لَهُ ثَمَرٌ** refers to the actual presence of fruit on their trees, as in summer. This is a view free from affectation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقَالَ لِصَاحِبِهِ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُ أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مَالًا وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا** — **...so he said to his companion while he was conversing with him, ‘I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue.’** [Qur'an 18:34]. Conversation (المحاورة) is addressing and responding in speech. The retinue (النفر) refers to individuals who accompany a person with a certain degree of closeness; they are called *nafar* because they go forth (*yanfirun*) with him. For this reason, some have interpreted it as servants and children, and others as a group or kinsfolk. The first is more in line with what Allah the Exalted will narrate of his companion's response: **إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ مِنْكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا** (If you see me as less than you in wealth and children), where he substituted "children" for "retinue." The meaning is that the one for whom We made the two gardens said to his companion while he was conversing with him: "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," meaning children and servants.

This which he said to his companion reflects a specific delusion he held, deviated from the truth. He looked at himself while having absolute disposal over what Allah had granted him of wealth and children, facing no interference in what he desired therein. Thus, he believed he was their owner. This was true in a sense, but he forgot that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who made him owner and is the true Owner of what He granted him. He forgot that these were things Allah had subjected to him and given him power over from the ornaments of the worldly life, which is a trial (فتنة) and a test (بلاء) by which man is examined so that Allah may distinguish the corrupt from the good. Rather, the ornament attracted his soul to itself, so he reckoned himself independent of his Lord, self-sufficient in what he possessed, and believed that all causal influence lay with the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) that were subjected to him.

Thus, he forgot Allah, the Glorified, and relied upon the causes; this is polytheism (الشرك). Then he turned to himself and saw that he was disposing of the causes, dominating them, so he thought this was due to his own nobility and was seized by pride, becoming arrogant toward his companion. To this refers the difference between the two descriptions: the description of the Exalted regarding his ownership when He said: **جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ** (We made for one of them two gardens...) and He did not say "one of them had two gardens," and the man's description of himself when he said to his companion: "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue." He saw only himself and forgot that his Lord was the One who gave him power over the wealth he had and honored him with the retinue he possessed. Thus, his saying to his companion, "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," follows the same course as Qarun's saying to those who advised him not to exult and to do good with what Allah had given him of wealth: **إِنَّمَا أُوتِيتُهُ عَلَى عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي** (I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have) [Qur'an 28:78].

This which is revealed by his saying, "I am wealthier than you," namely the claim of personal nobility and inherent entitlement, followed by polytheism through heedlessness of Allah and reliance upon apparent causes, is what he manifested when he entered his garden and said, as narrated by Allah: **مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذِهِ أَبَدًا وَمَا أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قَائِمَةً** (I do not think that this will ever perish, and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in...) [Qur'an 18:35-36].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُ وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ قَالَ** — **He entered his garden while he was wrongdoing himself. He said...** [Qur'an 18:35] until the end of the two verses. The four pronouns refer to the man. The word "garden" (الجنة) is used in the singular to refer to the genus, which is why it was not dualized. It has also been said that this is because entry cannot be realized in both gardens simultaneously; rather, it occurs in one after the other.

It is said in *al-Kashshaf*: "If you ask: Why did He singularize 'garden' after dualizing it? I say: Its meaning is that he entered that which was *his* garden—meaning he has no other garden besides it. That is, he has no portion in the Garden promised to the believers, so what he possessed in the world was his garden and nothing else; he did not intend the two gardens specifically nor just one of them." This is a subtle point.

His saying: **وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ** (while he was wrongdoing himself)—he was a wrongdoer because he was arrogant toward his companion when he said, "I am wealthier than you," which reveals his self-conceit and his polytheism through forgetting Allah and relying on apparent causes. All of these are destructive vices.

His saying: **قَالَ مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذِهِ أَبَدًا** (He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish’). Perishing (البيد والبيدودة) refers to destruction and annihilation. The demonstrative "this" refers to the garden. The sentence is detached because it is in the sense of a response to a hypothetical question; as if when it was said, "He entered his garden," it was asked, "What did he do?" and it was replied, "He said: I do not think that this will ever perish..."

He expressed the permanence of his garden by saying, "I do not think it will perish," and the negation of "thinking" (الظن) regarding a matter is an allusion to it being a supposition or possibility that is not even considered or inclined toward. Thus, the meaning of "I do not think this will perish" is that its permanence and duration are matters the soul is so tranquil about and certain of that it does not even hesitate or think about its perishing or suppose it will vanish.

This is the state of man: his soul does not attach itself to a vanishing thing insofar as it is changing and quickly passing; rather, the heart attaches itself to what it perceives in it of the mark of permanence (سمة البقاء), however it may be. Thus, he is drawn to it and does not turn away toward any possibility of its annihilation. You see him, when the world approaches him, becoming tranquil toward it, beginning to enjoy its ornaments, devoting himself to it, while his whims encompass him and his hopes lengthen, as if he does not see any end for himself, nor any vanishing for the blessing in his hand, nor any severance for the causes that have aided him. And you see him, when the world turns away from him, seized by despair and hopelessness, making him forget every hope for relief, and concluding that his misery and ill state will endure forever.

The reason for all this is what Allah has placed in his primordial nature (الفطرة) of attachment to this vanishing ornament as a trial and test. When he turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, he becomes cut off, sufficing with himself, the worldly ornaments before him, and the apparent causes surrounding him. He attaches himself to the present state he witnesses, and the attraction of ornaments and glitters calls him to freeze upon them and not pay attention to their vanishing—which is the claim of permanence. Whenever the strike of the primordial intellect (العقل الفطري) knocks at him—warning that time will betray him, causes will fail him, the goods of life will bid him farewell, and his deferred life will reach its term—the following of whims and long hopes prevent him from listening or paying heed.

This is the condition of the people of the world: they remain in a contradiction of opinion, doing what they believe with their whims while denying it with their intellects. However, they find tranquility in the opinion of whim, which prevents them from paying heed to the judgment of the intellect.

This is the meaning of their belief in the duration of apparent causes and the permanence of the ornaments of worldly life. For this reason, he said in what Allah narrated: **مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذِهِ أَبَدًا** (I do not think that this will ever perish) and he did not say, "This will never perish."

His saying: **وَمَا أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قَائِمَةً** (and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in) is based on what preceded regarding eternalization in his saying, "I do not think that this will ever perish." For this gives rise to the deemed impossibility (استبعاد) of the current state changing by the setting in of the Hour. All that Allah, the Glorified, has narrated of the arguments of the polytheists in denying the Resurrection is based on this deemed impossibility, such as their saying: **مَنْ يُحْيِي الْعِظَامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ** (Who will revive the bones when they have decayed?) [Qur'an 36:78] and their saying: **أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** (When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be created anew?) [Qur'an 32:10].

His saying: **وَلَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلَى رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِنْهَا مُنْقَلَبًا** (and even if I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find a resort better than this) [Qur'an 18:36] is based on what preceded of the claim of personal nobility and entitlement to good. This produces in man a false hope for every good and happiness without any deed that necessitates it. He says: It is unlikely that the Hour will set in, but if it does and I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find—by my own nobility (and he does not say "my Lord will give me")—a resort better than this garden to which I shall return.

This speaker's soul deceived him in the nobility it claimed, until he swore by what he said, as indicated by the *lam* of the oath in His saying: **وَلَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ** (and even if I am returned) and the *lam* of emphasis and the *nun* in His saying: **لَأَجِدَنَّ** (I will surely find). He said: **رُدِدْتُ** (I am returned) and did not say "my Lord returns me to Him," and he said: **لَأَجِدَنَّ** (I will surely find) and did not say "Allah gave me."

The two verses are like His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَاهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هَذَا لِي وَمَا أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قَائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلَى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنَى** (And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due! I do not think that the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best [reward] with Him.’) [Qur'an 41:50].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ لَهُ صَاحِبُهُ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُ أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاكَ رَجُلًا** — **His companion said to him, as he was conversing with him, ‘Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then fashioned you as a man?’** [Qur'an 18:37]. This verse and the three following it are a rebuttal from the man's companion, refuting his saying: "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," and then his saying when he entered his garden: "I do not think that this will ever perish." The speech, in terms of the speaker's intent, has been analyzed into two aspects: First, his exaltation over Allah, the Glorified, by claiming independence in himself and in what he possesses of wealth and retinue, and his self-aggrandizement through the power and strength he has. Second, his exaltation over his companion and his belittling of him for his lack of wealth and lowliness. He then refuted each of the two claims by severing their substance and uprooting them. Thus, his saying: **أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ** (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you...) until His saying: **إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ** (...except by Allah) is a rebuttal of the first claim, and his saying: **إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ** (If you see me as less...) until His saying: **طَلَبًا** (...to seek it) is a rebuttal of the second.

His saying: **قَالَ لَهُ صَاحِبُهُ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُ** (His companion said to him, as he was conversing with him)—the repetition of the phrase "as he was conversing with him" indicates that he did not deviate from his state of tranquil faith and dignity upon hearing what he heard from the man. Rather, he continued his conversation while maintaining its etiquette. Part of this etiquette was his gentleness with him in speech and not being harsh by mentioning what would be considered a prayer against him that would normally upset him. Thus, he did not mention his children with any ill, as he mentioned his garden; rather, he sufficed with what was alluded to in his mention of the garden—the possibility of it becoming a slippery plain and its water sinking away.

His saying: **أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ** (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you...)—the interrogation is for denunciation (الاستفهام للإنكار). He denounces what his speech contained of polytheism toward Allah, the Glorified, by claiming independence for himself and for causes and effects, as previously indicated. Among the branches of his polytheism was his deeming the setting in of the Hour as impossible and his hesitation regarding it.

As for what was mentioned in *al-Kashshaf*—that he deemed him a disbeliever in Allah and a denier of His blessings because of his doubt in the Resurrection, just as one who belies the Messenger is a disbeliever—this is not sound. How could it be so when he mentions in his rectification the negation of polytheism from himself? If it were as he said, he would have mentioned faith in the Return (المعاد).

If you say: The verses are explicit regarding the man's polytheism, and the polytheists deny the Return. I say: The man was not among the polytheists in the sense of idol-worshippers. He acknowledged during his speech what the principles of paganism do not allow; for he referred to Him, the Glorified, by saying: **رَبِّي** (my Lord), whereas the pagans do not see Him as a Lord for man or a worshipped deity; rather, He is to them the Lord of lords and God of gods. Nor did he deny the Return from its root, as previously indicated; rather, he hesitated and deemed it unlikely by turning away from reflecting upon it. Had he denied it, he would have said "If I were returned" and not "And even if (وَلَئِنْ) I am returned to my Lord."

Thus, the evil consequence mentioned for his affair in the verse is only due to his polytheism in the sense of forgetting his Lord and claiming independence for himself and for apparent causes. In this is the removal of Him, the Exalted, from Lordship (الربوبية) and the handing over of the reins of sovereignty and governance to other than Him. This is the root of corruption upon which every corrupt branch grows, whether he acknowledges monotheism (التوحيد) with his tongue or denies it and affirms other deities. Al-Zamakhshari said regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذَا أَبَدًا** (He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish’), and how excellent is what he said: "You see most of the wealthy among the Muslims, even if they do not utter such things with their tongues, the tongues of their states speak it and cry out with it."

This believer invalidated his companion's claim by saying: **أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاكَ رَجُلًا** (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then fashioned you as a man?) by drawing his attention to his origin, which is dust, then the drop of fluid. For that is the origin of man; whatever is added to that until man becomes a complete human being with attributes and effects is a pure gift from Allah. His origin possesses nothing of that, nor do any of the other apparent cosmic causes; for they are like man—they possess nothing of themselves or their own effects except by a gift from Allah, the Glorified.

So, what man possesses as a fashioned man—of humanity and its effects such as knowledge, life, power, and governance by which he subjects cosmic causes to reach his goals and needs—all of that is purely owned by Allah. He gave it to man and made him its owner, yet man did not thereby exit the ownership of Allah, nor was he cut off from Him. Rather, man was clothed with what he was clothed with from them, so they were attributed to him by His will. Had He not willed, man would not have owned any of that. Thus, man has no right to be independent of Him, the Exalted, in anything of himself or his effects, nor does any of the cosmic causes have that right.

He says: You are that dust, then that semen, which possessed nothing of humanity, manhood, or their effects. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who gave them to you by His will and made you their owner, and He is the true Owner of what He gave you. So why do you disbelieve in Him and conceal His Lordship? And where do you stand in relation to independence?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَكِنَّا هُوَ اللَّهُ رَبِّي وَلَا أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّي أَحَدًا** — **But as for me, He is Allah, my Lord, and I do not associate anyone with my Lord.** [Qur'an 18:38]. The famous recitation is *lakinna* (لَكِنَّا) with a short *a* on the doubled *nun* in continuous speech and its preservation in pause. Its origin, according to what they mentioned, is *lakin ana* (لكن أنا). The hamza was deleted after its vowel was transferred to the *nun*, and the *nun* was elided into the *nun*. Thus, the connection is with a doubled *nun* with a *fatha* without an *alif*, and the pause is with the *alif*, as in the first-person pronoun *ana* (أنا).

The word **رَبِّي** (my Lord) is repeated in the verse, the second being the placement of a manifest noun in place of a pronoun. The context would normally require "and I do not associate anyone with Him." This is to indicate the reason for the judgment by linking it to the attribute, as if he said: "and I do not associate anyone with Him because He is my Lord, and it is not permissible to associate anyone with Him due to His Lordship." This is a clarification of the believer's state in contrast to what the disbeliever claimed for himself, and the meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْلَا إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ** — **Why did you not say, when you entered your garden, ‘[This is] as Allah has wished! There is no power except by Allah!’?** [Qur'an 18:39]. This is a continuation of the believer's speech to his disbelieving companion. It is an exhortation and a rebuke to his companion for saying when he entered his garden: "I do not think that this will ever perish." He should have replaced it with his saying: "As Allah has wished! There is no power except by Allah!" thus attributing the entire matter to the will of Allah and restricting power to Him, based on what he had clarified to him—that every blessing is by the will of Allah and there is no power except by Him.

His saying: **مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ** (As Allah has wished) is either on the assumption of: "The matter is what Allah has wished," or: "What Allah has wished is [existent]." In both cases, *ma* (مَا) is a relative pronoun. It is also possible for it to be conditional, the assumption being: "Whatever Allah wished, came to be." The most appropriate to the context is the first assumption, because the purpose is to clarify the return of all matters to the will of Allah, the Exalted, in contrast to one who claims independence and self-sufficiency.

His saying: **لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ** (There is no power except by Allah) signifies that power subsists through Allah and restricts all power to Him, in the sense that whatever power appears in His creatures is itself subsisting through Him, without what He has given of it being cut off so that the creature becomes independent with it. The Exalted said: **أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** (...that power belongs entirely to Allah) [Qur'an 2:165].

With this, the response to what the disbeliever said to his companion and what he said upon entering his garden is complete.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ مِنْكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا فَعَسَى** — **If you see me as less than you in wealth and children, maybe...** [Qur'an 18:39] until the end of the two verses. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: The origin of *husban* (حُسْبَان) is the arrows that are shot to run in a single course, which was from the shooting of the knights. The root of the word is *hisab* (calculation), and that which is shot is called *husban* because it is as numerous as a calculation. He said: *Zalaq* (زَلَق) is smooth, level ground with no vegetation or anything else on it. The root of *zalaq* is that which feet slip from and do not remain firm upon.

It has preceded that the *sa‘id* (صَعِيد) is the surface of the earth when it is level with no vegetation on it. The meaning of the water becoming *ghawr* (غَوْر) is its becoming sunken, disappearing into the depths of the earth.

The two verses, as previously indicated, are a rebuttal from the believer to his disbelieving companion regarding his exaltation over him by being wealthier and having a stronger retinue. The rebuttal he presented is derived from his previous clarification, and its summary is: Since matters are by the will and power of Allah, and He has made you wealthier than me and stronger in retinue, the matter in that belongs to Him, not to you, such that you should boast and exalt yourself over me. Thus, it is possible and hoped that He will give me better than your garden and destroy your garden, turning me to a state better than your state today and turning you to a state worse than my state today, making me richer than you relative to myself and making you poorer than me relative to yourself.

It appears that *taran* (تَرَنِ) in His saying: **إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ** (If you see me as less...) is from *ra’y* (opinion) in the sense of belief, thus being one of the "verbs of the heart." The word **أَنَا** (I) is a separating pronoun (ضمير فصل) placed between its two objects, which were originally a subject and predicate. It is also possible for it to be from *ru’yah* in the sense of sight, in which case *ana* is a nominative pronoun emphasizing the elided object of *taran*.

The meaning of the verse is: If you see me as less than you in wealth and children, there is no harm, for the matter in that belongs to my Lord. Perhaps my Lord will give me better than your garden and send upon it—that is, upon your garden—missiles (مرامي) of His heavenly punishment, such as hail, a scorching wind, a thunderbolt, or the like, so it becomes empty, smooth ground with no trees or crops. Or its water becomes sunken, so you will never be able to seek it due to its deep penetration into the ground.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ** — **And his produce was encompassed [by destruction], and he began to wring his hands...** [Qur'an 18:42]. Encompassing (الإحاطة) a thing is an allusion to its destruction; it is taken from the enemy's surrounding and encircling it from all sides such that it is cut off from every helper and supporter, which is destruction. The Exalted said: **وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ** (...and they think they are encompassed...) [Qur'an 10:22].

His saying: **فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ** (and he began to wring his hands) is an allusion to regret, for the regretful person often turns his hands over, back to front. His saying: **وَهِيَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَى عُرُوشِهَا** (while it lay fallen on its trellises) is an allusion to complete ruin, as has been said; for ruined, demolished houses first have their trellises—which are their roofs—fall to the ground, then their walls fall upon the fallen trellises. *Khaway* (الخوي) means falling, and it was said the root meaning is emptiness.

His saying: **وَيَقُولُ يَا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّي أَحَدًا** (and saying, ‘I wish I had not ascribed any partner to my Lord!’) means: I wish I had not attached myself to what I attached myself to, nor relied upon nor found tranquility in these causes which I reckoned had independence in influence, and that I had returned the entire matter to my Lord; for my effort has gone astray and my soul has perished.

The meaning is: All types of his wealth were destroyed or the fruit of his garden was spoiled, so he became regretful over the wealth he had spent while the garden lay in ruins, saying: I wish I had not associated anyone with my Lord, nor found repose in what I found repose in and was deceived by—of myself and other causes—which have benefited me nothing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ فِئَةٌ يَنْصُرُونَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مُنْتَصِرًا** — **He had no party to help him besides Allah, nor could he help himself.** [Qur'an 18:43]. A party (الفئة) is a group, and one who helps himself (المنتصر) is one who is immune or defended.

Just as the first five verses—namely His saying: **قَالَ لَهُ صَاحِبُهُ** (His companion said to him...) until His saying: **طَلَبًا** (...to seek it)—were a verbal clarification of the man's error in his disbelief and polytheism, so too are these two verses—namely His saying: **وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ** (And his produce was encompassed...) until His saying: **وَمَا كَانَ مُنْتَصِرًا** (...nor could he help himself)—an actual, practical clarification for him. As for his attachment to the duration of the world and the continuity of its ornament in his saying: "I do not think that this will ever perish," the error in it was made manifest to him when his produce was encompassed and his garden became fallen on its trellises. As for his repose in causes and reliance upon them when he said to his companion, "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," his error in it was clarified by His saying: "He had no party to help him besides Allah." As for the claim of his independence in himself and his boasting of it, the aspect of its invalidity was alluded to by His saying: "nor could he help himself."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُنَالِكَ الْوَلَايَةُ لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا** — **There, all authority belongs to Allah, the Real. He is best in rewarding and best in [providing] a destination.** [Qur'an 18:44]. The famous recitation is *al-walayah* (الْوَلَايَةُ) with a *fatha* on the *waw*, and it has been recited with a *kasra*; the meaning is the same. Some mentioned that with a *fatha* it means support (النصرة) and with a *kasra* it means authority (السلطان), but this is not established. Likewise, **الْحَقِّ** (the Real) is with a *jar*. Reward (الثواب) is the general consequence and wage, and it has become predominant for a good and beautiful wage. *‘Uqub* (عُقْبًا) with a *damma* then *sukun*, or with two *dammas*, means the end or destination (العاقبة).

The commentators mentioned that the allusion in His saying: **هُنَالِكَ** (There) is to the meaning of His saying: **أُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ** (his produce was encompassed); that is, in that place or at that time—which is the place and time of destruction—authority belongs to Allah. They say *al-walayah* means support (النصرة); that is, Allah, the Glorified, is the Supporter of man when affliction encompasses him and he is cut off from all causes; there is no supporter besides Him.

This is a true meaning in itself, but it does not suit the purpose for which the verses were sent down, which is to clarify that the entire matter belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and He is the Creator of everything and the Governor of every affair, and for other than Him there is nothing but the mirage of illusion and the beautification of life for the purpose of trial and examination. Had it been as they mentioned, it would have been more appropriate to describe Him, the Exalted, in His saying: **لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ** (to Allah, the Real) with power, might, ability, dominance, and the like, not with something like "the Real" (الحق) which stands in contrast to "the False" (الباطل). Also, there would be no apparent reason or beautiful placement for His saying: **هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا** (He is best in rewarding and best in [providing] a destination).

The truth, and Allah knows best, is that authority (الولاية) means the ownership of governance (مالكية التدبير), which is the meaning permeating all its derivations, as passed in the discussion on His saying: **إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ** (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle...) [Qur'an 5:55]. That is, when destruction encompasses and causes fail to have influence, and the helplessness of man—who used to see himself as independent and self-sufficient—becomes clear, the authority over the affair of man and everything, and the ownership of its governance, belongs to Allah. This is because He is a Real God (إله حق) to whom belongs governance and influence according to the reality of the matter. Other than Him, from the apparent causes called partners with Him in governance and influence, are false (باطل) in themselves, possessing nothing of effect except what Allah has permitted them and made them own. They have no independence except in name according to what man imagined; thus, they are false in themselves, real through Allah, the Glorified, and Allah is the Real by His essence, the Independent, the Self-Sufficient in Himself.

When a comparison is made between Him—exalted is He above comparison—and other causes called partners in influence, Allah, the Glorified, is better than them in reward; for He rewards the one who submits to Him with a true reward, while they reward the one who submits to them and attaches himself to them with a false, vanishing reward that does not endure—and even that is from Allah and by His permission. And Allah, the Glorified, is better than them in destination (عاقبة) because He, the Glorified, is the Fixed Truth who does not perish, vanish, or change from what He is of Majesty and Honor. These other things are vanishing, changing matters that Allah made as an ornament for the worldly life, with which man becomes infatuated and to which his heart attaches, until the decree reaches its term, and Allah will surely make them a barren plain.

Since man cannot do without attaching himself to something to which he attributes governance and from which he expects the rectification of his state, his Lord is better for him than any other, because He is best in rewarding and best in destination.

Some mentioned that the allusion in His saying: **هُنَالِكَ** (There) is to the Day of Resurrection, so the meaning of reward and destination would be what is on that day. The context, as you know, does not support any of that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلَ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَاءٍ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ** — **Draw for them the parable of the life of this world: [It is] like the water We send down from the sky...** [Qur'an 18:45]. This is the second parable, struck to represent the worldly life with its accompanying ornament that is swift to vanish.

*Hashim* (هَشِيم) is a passive participle from *hashm*, which, as al-Raghib said, is the breaking of a soft thing like a plant. *Dhara yadhru dharwan* (ذَرَا يَذْرُو ذَرْوًا) means to scatter; it was also said it means to bring and take away. His saying: **فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَبَاتُ الْأَرْضِ** (and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it) and He did not say "it mingled with the vegetation of the earth" to indicate its dominance in the formation of the plant over other parts. He did not mention with the water of the sky other waters from springs and rivers because the origin of all is the rain water. His saying: **فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا** (then it becomes chaff)—"becomes" (*asbaha*) here, as has been said, is in the sense of *sara* (to become), so it does not restrict the report to the morning.

The meaning is: Strike for these people who are infatuated with the ornament of the world and turning away from the remembrance of their Lord the parable of the worldly life: like water We sent down from the sky—which is rain—and the vegetation of the earth mingled with it, so it flourished with freshness and joy and appeared in the most beautiful attire. Then after that, it became broken, severed chaff that the winds play with, scattering it and bringing it and taking it away. And Allah has power over all things.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الْمَالُ وَالْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — **Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world...** [Qur'an 18:46]. The verse is like a conclusion to the previous parable, which is that wealth and children—even if hearts attach to them and souls long for them, expecting benefit from them and surrounding them with hopes—are a swiftly vanishing, deceptive ornament. They cannot reward him or benefit him in all that he desired from them, nor can they fulfill all that he hopes and wishes for, nor even most of it. In the verse—as you see—is a return to the beginning of the discourse, namely His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَهَا** (Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it...) [Qur'an 18:7-8].

His saying: **وَالْبَاقِيَاتُ الصَّالِحَاتُ خَيْرٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلًا** (but lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward and better in hope). What is meant by lasting righteous deeds (الباقيات الصالحات) are righteous acts; for the deeds of man are preserved for him with Allah by the text of the Qur'an, so they are lasting. If they are righteous, they are lasting righteous deeds. They are better with Allah in reward because Allah rewards the person who brings them with the best reward. And they are better in hope because what is hoped for through them of Allah's mercy and honor is attainable for man; thus, they are truer in hope than the ornaments and glitters of the world, which do not fulfill for man most of what they promise. The hopes attached to the world are mostly false, and what is true among them is deceptive and misleading.

Several narrations have been reported through the paths of the Shi‘a and the Sunnis from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and through the paths of the Shi‘a from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), stating that the lasting righteous deeds are the four glorifications (التسبيحات الأربع): *Subhan Allah, wal-Hamdu lillah, wa la ilaha illallah, wallahu Akbar*. In another, it is the prayer (الصلاة), and in another, the love for the Ahl al-Bayt. All of these are of the nature of applying the concept to its prime examples (الجري والانطباق).
